items.q: Why can you not just ignore umlauts in German?
items.correct: They change the pronunciation AND meaning of words.
items.random_wrong: They are pronounced the same as without dots.
items.why: Students treat umlauts as optional accents. They are CHANGED VOWELS. Schon (already) vs schön (beautiful) are different words. Mutter (mother) vs Mütter (mothers) are singular vs plural.

items.misconception: Accusative (because it sounds like a direct relationship).
items.why: Students guess case based on logic. Mit is a DATIVE preposition - one of several you memorise (aus, bei, mit, nach, seit, von, zu - mnemonic). Each preposition has a fixed case.
Students use English -s plural. German has multiple plural patterns including UMLAUT + er for many masculine nouns: Mann to Männer, Buch to Bücher, Land to Länder. Plurals must often be memorised.
The answer examiners want is: Volksabstimmung. “Wahlmann” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: Gemeinsamkeit. “Unterschied” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did Switzerland adopt women's suffrage at federal level?
items.why: The real figure is 1971. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
